figures.caption: Fig. 4: Pareto-front comparison for the rigid gripper case study. (Left) Monolithic exact frontier (3 vertices) versus MCDPL optimistic approximations at resolutions R ∈ {5, 10, 20, 40}. (Right) IGD convergence versus computational time: the normalised Inverted Generational Distance of MCDPL optimistic solutions decreases monotonically with increasing resolution, while the exact monolithic solver achieves IGD= 0.

Fig. 6: Pareto-front comparison for the rover case study. (a) LDP tangent-plane approximation at several N vs. ground truth; (b) zoomed view highlighting convergence in the cost range 1000–1200 USD; (c) MCDPL optimistic approximations at resolutions R ∈ {5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80}; (d) best available result from each method compared against the ground-truth frontier.

TABLE IV: Rover case study: accuracy–time comparison across methods. For Methods III–IV, δ = 0 holds by Theorem 10. The column ∆HVrel reports a one-sided normalized hypervolume gap with respect to the reference frontier C: hypervolume deficit (HV (C)−HV (P−))/HV (C) for inner fronts and hypervolume excess (HV (P+)−HV (C))/HV (C) for optimistic fronts. For MCDPL pessimistic rows, the coverage column corresponds to e[C,P−].

Fig. 7: Convergence and efficiency comparison. (a) Directional excess metrics versus precision parameter: e[P, C] and e[C,P] for LDP, e[P+, C] for MCDPL optimistic, and e[C,P−] for MCDPL pessimistic; (b) the same directional excess metrics plotted against computation time, showing that LDP achieves markedly lower error at a fraction of the MCDPL runtime.
